The Sedona Conference

2000–2025 "Wrapped"

An analysis of Sedona Conference citations found in court filings, judicial opinions, and legal scholarship from 2000 to 2025.

Based on citations available through CourtListener/RECAP (federal court filings), Westlaw (judicial opinions and briefs), and SSRN (academic papers). Data reflects publicly accessible records and may not capture all citations.

Methodology
1

Data Collection

Citation data was gathered from three complementary legal research sources. CourtListener/RECAP provided federal court filings via their public REST API. We queried for documents containing "Sedona" references and downloaded 2,290 PDF documents from the RECAP archive. Westlaw exports supplied judicial opinions, trial court briefs, and expert materials in structured CSV format, totaling over 2,200 records with associated case metadata. Finally, SSRN (Social Science Research Network) provided 139 academic papers and legal scholarship through API queries, including abstracts and publication metadata.

2

Text Extraction

Documents were converted into machine-readable text for analysis. PDF court filings were processed using PyMuPDF for text extraction, with OCR (optical character recognition) applied as a fallback for scanned documents. Text was then normalized to handle encoding variations and remove formatting artifacts. Metadata fields (including filing dates, court names, case numbers, judge names, and author information) were parsed and standardized across all sources.

3

AI Classification

Each document was analyzed using large language models (Claude and GPT-4) to identify and categorize citations. The system first identified passages containing "Sedona" mentions and extracted surrounding context (typically 500-1000 characters). This context was then sent to the LLM with structured prompts to determine: the specific Sedona publication being cited, any principle numbers referenced (for Sedona Principles citations), and the nature of the citation. The LLM returned structured JSON responses mapping each citation to one of 18 publication categories corresponding to Sedona's major works.

4

Data Aggregation

Classified results from all sources were merged into a unified dataset for analysis. Fields from each source (RECAP, Westlaw, SSRN) were mapped to a common schema with consistent naming conventions. Duplicate citations appearing across multiple sources were identified and merged using case numbers and document titles. Publications were mapped to their corresponding Sedona Conference Working Groups (WG1-WG13), and date formats were standardized to enable temporal analysis.

5

Visualization

The final dataset was transformed into this interactive dashboard using Python for data processing and Plotly.js for visualization. Citations were aggregated by year, quarter, publication, court, and other dimensions. The resulting charts (including stacked bar charts, area charts, and cumulative growth curves) were compiled into a single-page HTML document with embedded data and responsive styling for accessibility across devices.

Limitations

This analysis captures citations available through the searched databases and may not represent all Sedona Conference citations in existence. Classification accuracy depends on document quality and clarity of citation context. Some older documents may have OCR artifacts affecting text extraction.
